# crimes

> crimes is an open-source command-line tool that scans TypeScript and
> JavaScript repositories for change risk and AI-agent risk. It runs
> locally with no cloud calls, ships a stable versioned JSON contract
> so AI coding agents can consume its output safely, and is MIT-licensed.

crimes is positioned narrowly. It is not a linter (ESLint, Biome),
not a security scanner (Semgrep, CodeQL), and not a code-quality
platform (SonarQube). Its wedge is local, open-source, agent-native
codebase risk and context — the question "where is future change most
likely to go wrong, and what should a human or AI agent know before
editing it?".

Core principles:
- Deterministic before magical. Detectors must work without any LLM.
- Evidence before judgement. Every finding cites concrete AST or git
  evidence (line ranges, churn counts, import patterns).
- JSON output is the product contract. Every report carries a
  `schema_version` and a `report_type` discriminator
  (`"scan"`, `"context"`, `"hotspots"`, `"diff"`, `"baseline"`,
  `"baseline_check"`, `"verdict"`). Breaking changes bump
  `schema_version`.
- Built for AI agents. Ships a Claude Code skill and AGENTS.md so any
  agent (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor, Aider, Continue, Copilot
  Workspace) can use it without per-prompt setup.

## Install

- npm: `npm install -g crimes`
- one-shot: `npx crimes scan .`
- requires Node.js 18+

## Commands (v0.3.0 — release candidate; latest published v0.2.0)

Per-file / per-directory (shipped in v0.1.0):
- `crimes scan .` — scan repo, print top findings
- `crimes scan . --format json` — emit the versioned JSON contract
- `crimes scan --changed --format json` — working-tree-only post-edit scan
- `crimes context <file> --format json` — pre-edit briefing for one file
- `crimes hotspots --since 90d --format json` — churn × findings ranking

Branch / PR safety (shipped in v0.2.0):
- `crimes scan --changed --fail-on low|medium|high` — exit 1 when the
  changed set has a finding at or above the threshold. Only valid with
  `--changed`. JSON gains `fail_on` and `failed` fields.
- `crimes diff <base...head> --format json` — new / fixed / unchanged
  crimes between two committed refs (triple-dot range). Working-tree-safe;
  exports each ref via `git archive` into a temp dir. Findings matched
  by stable fingerprint `<type>::<file>::<symbol-or-empty>`.
- `crimes baseline save` — snapshot current findings to
  `.crimes/baseline.json` (intended to be committed).
- `crimes baseline check --fail-on low|medium|high` — re-scan and fail
  only on findings absent from the baseline. Default threshold `medium`.
- `crimes verdict --base origin/main --format json` — one-line "did
  this branch make the repo cleaner, worse, unchanged, or mixed?"
  Advisory by default; opt into a hard gate with `--fail-on worse |
  new-high | new-medium`.

## CI integration (v0.2.0)

Three deterministic gating modes — all share a uniform exit-code
contract: `0` success, `1` configured `--fail-on` threshold met,
`2` usage / environment error.

- Mode A — `crimes scan --changed --fail-on high` (narrow,
  changed-files-only).
- Mode B — `crimes baseline check --fail-on medium` (legacy repo;
  pin debt with a committed `.crimes/baseline.json`).
- Mode C — `crimes verdict --base origin/main --fail-on new-high`
  (PR summary that flips to a hard gate on any new high finding).

Copy-paste GitHub Actions workflow ships in the repo at
`examples/github-actions/crimes.yml`. Full integration guide:
`docs/ci.md`.

## Structural detectors shipped today (v0.1.0)

- `large_function` (charge: God Function) — functions over a body-line threshold
- `large_file` (charge: God File) — files over a line threshold
- `todo_density` (charge: Unfinished Business) — clusters of TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK
- `direct_date` (charge: Temporal Recklessness) — `Date.now()` / `new Date()`
  used directly in domain code

## Information architecture detectors (new in v0.3.0)

These five IA detectors are new in v0.3.0. They are deterministic:
**no LLM, no API key, and no network access** is required. Every
finding cites concrete files, line numbers, and string literals;
cross-file findings populate `related_files` and render as an "Also
touches:" block in the human report.

- `missing_agent_context` (charge: Missing Agent Context) — repos that
  declare a `bin` in `package.json` but ship no `AGENTS.md`,
  `CLAUDE.md`, or `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`.
- `route_metadata_drift` (charge: Route Metadata Drift) — route path,
  file location, default-export component name, `<title>`,
  `metadata.title`, and nav-source labels disagree for the same
  destination (≥3-source quorum).
- `duplicated_navigation_source` (charge: Duplicated Navigation Source)
  — a single internal destination appears in two or more nav-like
  sources with different non-empty labels.
- `concept_alias_drift` (charge: Concept Alias Drift) — multiple
  aliases from a seeded concept group (`team` / `workspace` / `org`;
  `plan` / `subscription` / `tier`; etc.) appear across the product
  surface, each in ≥2 distinct directories. Capped at the three
  strongest groups per scan.
- `docs_code_drift` (charge: Docs-Code Drift) — a markdown doc under
  `docs/` (or a root-level `*.md`) contains a local link that does not
  resolve on disk.

IA findings phrase summaries as "appears to" / "may" — they are
**ambiguity signals**, not claims of semantic truth. The
interpretation belongs to the human or agent reading the report. Full
reference (quorum rules, false-positive notes, suggested fixes):
`docs/finding-types/ia.md`.

Every finding (structural and IA) includes typed scores: `severity`,
`confidence`, and `agent_risk`. Default ranking is by aggregate risk,
so multiple sources of truth, weak tests, and hidden side effects can
elevate a finding even when local severity is low.

## Agent integration

- Claude Code: `.claude/skills/crimes/SKILL.md` auto-loads in any repo.
- Other agents: a top-level `AGENTS.md` is read by OpenAI Codex CLI,
  Cursor, Aider, Continue, and GitHub Copilot Workspace.

Recommended loop for any agent:
1. Before editing a file:   `crimes context <file> --format json`
2. After editing:           `crimes scan --changed --format json`
3. Before opening the PR:   `crimes verdict --base origin/main --fail-on new-high`
4. Triaging the repo:       `crimes hotspots --format json`
5. Any new `severity: "high"` finding introduced by the edit is a blocker.

## Shipped in v0.3.0 — information architecture crimes

v0.2.0 made crimes useful for branches, PRs, CI, and agent loops.
v0.3.0 is focused on detecting repo structure drift that confuses
humans, coding agents, teams, and customers — source-of-truth and
concept drift across code, routes, navigation, copy, docs, and
ownership. The five IA detectors listed above are the headline slice.
`packages/cli/package.json` is bumped to `0.3.0`; the npm publish
fires once the GitHub Release tagged `v0.3.0` triggers the release
workflow.

## IA candidates deferred from v0.3.0

These remain in the long-term IA roadmap and are **not implemented**.
Do not invoke or document them as available.

- `orphaned_destination` (charge: Orphaned Destination) — pages /
  routes / screens unreachable from primary navigation, route
  registries, sitemap metadata, or internal links. Needs route
  discovery to mature.
- `parallel_destination` (charge: Parallel Destination) — multiple
  pages or flows that appear to serve the same user intent
  (`/billing` vs `/settings/billing` vs `/account/subscription`;
  `InviteUserModal` vs `AddTeamMemberDialog`). Needs near-duplicate
  scoring to avoid noisy guesses.
- `permission_ia_drift` (charge: Permission IA Drift) — navigation,
  route guards, docs, and policy code describe access using different
  roles or concepts. Requires policy / route-guard discovery.
- `action_label_drift` (charge: Action Label Drift) — the same action
  or object is labelled differently across UI copy and code
  ("Delete" / "Remove" / "Archive"; "User" / "Member" / "Seat").
- Command-drift variant of `docs_code_drift` — docs that reference a
  CLI command the published `bin` no longer implements. Needs
  deterministic command-registration scanning that is not yet shipped.

## Next-target candidate — v0.4.0: suppressions and config (tentative)

Recommended headline for the next release: `crimes init` +
`crimes.config.json` plumbing, `crimes ignore <id>` +
`.crimes/suppressions.json`, and the long-deferred
`crimes explain <id>` and `crimes diff --fail-on new-high`. Unlocks
adoption of the IA detectors on real repos by letting teams
scope-silence legitimate alias choices without disabling a detector
globally. Not yet implemented.

## Not shipped yet — do not invoke or document as available

- `crimes diff --fail-on new-high` — deferred.
- `crimes ignore <id>` + `.crimes/suppressions.json` — deferred (v0.4.0 candidate).
- `crimes explain <id>` — deferred (v0.4.0 candidate).
- `crimes init` + config plumbing — deferred (v0.4.0 candidate).
- Per-finding `scores.churn` / `scores.test_gap` / `scores.blast_radius` — deferred to v0.4.0+.
- `crimes ask` / LLM-assisted features — v1+.
- Dependency-graph + duplication detectors — v0.4.0+.
- Frontend / UI agent-risk detectors — v0.4.0+.
- Homebrew tap + standalone binaries — deferred.
